name: ML Policy Researcher
interests: EU AI Act and model governance
AI export controls and compute restrictions
persona_summary: A machine learning policy researcher focused on AI governance — specifically the EU AI Act implementation, frontier model red-teaming, alignment research from labs like Anthropic/DeepMind, and AI-related export controls. Not interested in consumer AI products, generic 'AI will change everything' pieces, or individual chatbot launches unless they intersect with policy.

qa_pairs.question: What enforcement tier does the EU AI Act assign to general-purpose AI models with systemic risk?
qa_pairs.article_title: EU AI Act tiered obligations for general-purpose models clarified
qa_pairs.article_content: The European Commission's latest guidance distinguishes two tiers of general-purpose AI models under the AI Act. The baseline tier requires technical documentation, copyright compliance measures, and transparent training data summaries. The 'systemic risk' tier, triggered when a model's cumulative training compute exceeds 10^25 FLOP, additionally requires model evaluations, adversarial testing, incident reporting, and cybersecurity controls. The Commission confirmed that open-source models are exempt from the baseline tier obligations unless they meet the systemic-risk threshold.

qa_pairs.question: How much compute did the new export control rule restrict for H100 shipments?
qa_pairs.article_title: US tightens AI chip export controls targeting advanced training runs
qa_pairs.article_content: The US Commerce Department's updated rule lowered the per-shipment threshold for H100 and H200 GPUs bound for Tier-2 countries to the equivalent of 1,700 H100s of aggregate performance, down from 3,100 previously. Licenses are required above that threshold. The rule also expanded the scope to include networking equipment supporting training clusters, and introduced a new 'verified end user' program to streamline shipments to qualified research institutions.

name: Casual Tech Consumer
persona_summary: A casual tech reader who follows Apple and consumer gadget news — new iPhone launches, Mac reviews, HomePod and smart-home products, and the broader Apple ecosystem. Not interested in enterprise technology, AI research papers, or deep policy analysis. Prefers product-focused, approachable writing.

qa_pairs.question: What is the battery life improvement on the new MacBook Pro?
qa_pairs.article_title: MacBook Pro refresh: M5 chip and a 24-hour battery claim
qa_pairs.article_content: Apple's updated MacBook Pro line, announced today, centers on the new M5 chip and a claimed 24 hours of battery life for video playback — up from 22 hours on the previous generation. The M5 uses a second-generation 3nm process and Apple says it delivers a 20% CPU and 30% GPU improvement over the M4. External display support expands to three 6K monitors on the Pro and Max variants. Pricing starts at $1,999 for the 14-inch base configuration.

name: NBA Trade Deadline Obsessive
interests: NBA trade deadline and roster moves
player contract and luxury tax mechanics
persona_summary: An NBA fan hyper-focused on the trade deadline, salary-cap mechanics, and the Eastern Conference playoff race. Wants transaction news, cap implications, and contract details. Not interested in game recaps, college basketball, player personal life stories, or other sports.

qa_pairs.question: What picks did the Knicks send out in the latest trade?
qa_pairs.article_title: Knicks acquire veteran wing; send out two first-rounders and a pick swap
qa_pairs.article_content: The New York Knicks finalized a trade this afternoon for a veteran wing, sending out an unprotected 2028 first-round pick, a lottery-protected 2026 first-round pick, and a 2027 first-round pick swap. The deal also includes a second-round pick heading the other way. The acquisition pushes the Knicks approximately $4 million into the luxury tax and hard-caps them at the second apron for the rest of the season, eliminating their ability to aggregate salaries in further trades.

name: Climate Finance Analyst
interests: carbon markets and voluntary offset integrity
green bond issuance and transition finance
persona_summary: A climate finance professional tracking carbon-market mechanics, voluntary offset integrity and quality standards, SEC and EU CSRD climate-disclosure regulations, and green/transition bond issuance. Does NOT want general environment-and-nature stories, pure climate science papers, or consumer sustainability lifestyle content.

qa_pairs.question: Which carbon methodology did the ICVCM reject from its Core Carbon Principles?
qa_pairs.article_title: ICVCM rejects REDD+ avoided-deforestation methodology from Core Carbon Principles
qa_pairs.article_content: The Integrity Council for the Voluntary Carbon Market announced today that it has declined to label three REDD+ avoided-deforestation methodologies as compliant with its Core Carbon Principles, citing concerns about baseline-setting, leakage accounting, and permanence. The decision affects roughly 30% of credits currently issued under Verra's VCS registry. Market reaction saw prices for unlabeled REDD+ credits fall sharply, while ICVCM-labeled credits traded at a widening premium.

name: Indie Game Developer
interests: indie game studio funding and closures
persona_summary: An indie game developer following Unity/Unreal engine releases and pricing changes, Steam and Epic store policy and marketplace mechanics, and funding/closure news for small and mid-size studios. Not interested in AAA game reviews, esports tournaments, console hardware announcements, or mobile-game ad-tech news.

qa_pairs.question: What revenue threshold did Unity set for the new runtime fee?
qa_pairs.article_title: Unity rolls back runtime fee, introduces a revenue share above $1M threshold
qa_pairs.article_content: Unity today confirmed it is retiring the controversial per-install 'runtime fee' announced in 2023 and replacing it with a flat 2.5% revenue share on titles earning above $1 million in trailing-twelve-month revenue. Developers on Unity Personal remain exempt. The policy applies only to games first shipped on Unity 6 or later; existing projects on earlier versions are grandfathered indefinitely under their original license terms. Developers responded cautiously, noting concerns about the accounting overhead of tracking revenue retroactively.
